title: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8
authors: Camille Nelson Kotton, Martin S. Hirsch

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding CMV Prevalence and Transmission Dynamics
items.content: CMV is a globally prevalent virus with varying seropositivity rates depending on socioeconomic status. In low- and middle-income regions, nearly all adults are seropositive, while in the U.S. and Canada, only half of adults show seropositivity.
keyPoints: Worldwide distribution of CMV
Seropositivity near 100% in many low- and middle-income regions
Approximately 50% seropositive in the United States and Canada
Prevalence of congenital CMV is estimated at 0.67%, with higher rates (1.42%) in low- and middle-income countries compared to high-income countries (0.48%)
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Transmission Pathways of CMV in Different Populations
items.content: CMV transmission requires prolonged or repeated intimate contact. It is common among young children in communal settings like day-care centers and can be transmitted from mother to fetus.
keyPoints: Not spread by casual contact; needs repeated or prolonged exposure
Common transmission routes: perinatal, early childhood (e.g., day-care), sexual contact
50% of nonimmune family members may seroconvert within 6 months when exposed to an infected child
CMV can be present in breast milk, saliva, feces, and urine
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: CMV Reactivation in Immunocompromised Individuals
items.content: In immunocompromised individuals, CMV reactivation is more common than primary infection. This includes those with HIV, organ transplant recipients, or patients with lymphoid neoplasms.
keyPoints: Reactivation more frequent when T lymphocyte-mediated immunity is compromised
Common in organ transplantation, HIV infection, and critical illness
Primary CMV infections in transplant recipients often from graft or blood products
CMV-seropositive transplant recipients may experience reactivation of latent virus
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Congenital CMV Infection and Maternal Primary Infection
items.content: Congenital CMV infection severity is largely linked to primary maternal infection, with deficient antibody production and T cell response associated with more severe disease.
keyPoints: Primary maternal infection correlates with congenital CMV disease severity.
Deficient capacity for precipitating antibodies and T cell responses leads to severe congenital CMV disease.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: CMV Reactivation in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: In immunosuppressed individuals, latent CMV can reactivate, leading to various syndromes. This is particularly common with potent T cell suppressants like antithymocyte globulin and alemtuzumab.
keyPoints: Latent CMV reactivation occurs when T cell responses are compromised.
Antithymocyte globulin and alemtuzumab increase risk of clinical CMV syndromes due to potent T cell suppression.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Characteristic Pathology of CMV Infection
items.content: CMV-infected cells, known as cytomegalic cells, are enlarged and contain an 'owl's eye' intranuclear inclusion. These cells can be found in various organs.
keyPoints: Cytomegalic cells are 2 to 4 times larger than surrounding cells.
Intranuclear inclusion measures 8-10 μm with a clear halo, resembling an 'owl's eye'.
Cytomegalic cells present in multiple organs including salivary glands and CNS.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Immunopathologic Reactions in CMV Disease
items.content: CMV infection can lead to immune complex formation, contributing to glomerulopathy, particularly post-renal transplantation.
keyPoints: Immune complexes detected in infected infants and associated with CMV-related glomerulopathies.
Post-transplantation patients may develop immune-complex glomerulopathy due to CMV.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.title: Congenital CMV Infection: Key Clinical Features and Complications
items.content: Congenital CMV infection can present with a range of symptoms from subclinical to severe. The most common presenting features include petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, and jaundice (60–80% of cases). Severe infections may lead to sensorineural complications in 40–50% of first-trimester infections, microcephaly with cerebral calcifications, intrauterine growth retardation, prematurity (30–50%), and other less common features like inguinal hernias and chorioretinitis.
keyPoints: CMV seroconversion rates during pregnancy: 1% to 7%
Clinical manifestations in infants from primary CMV infections: 5–20%
Mortality rate for affected infants: ~5%
Common presenting features: petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice (60–80%)
Sensorineural complications in first-trimester infections: up to 40–50%
Microcephaly with cerebral calcifications, intrauterine growth retardation, prematurity: 30–50%
Prognosis for severely infected infants is poor; many have intellectual or hearing difficulties
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Perinatal CMV Infection: Transmission and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Perinatal CMV infection can occur during delivery through an infected birth canal or postnatally via breast milk. Infants breast-fed for >1 month by seropositive mothers have a 40–60% chance of becoming infected. While most perinatally acquired infections remain asymptomatic, some may present with protracted interstitial pneumonitis, especially in premature infants, and other symptoms like poor weight gain, adenopathy, rash, hepatitis, anemia, and atypical lymphocytosis.
keyPoints: CMV infection risk from breast-feeding by seropositive mothers: 40–60%
Most perinatally acquired infections are asymptomatic
Protracted interstitial pneumonitis associated with CMV in premature infants
Other symptoms: poor weight gain, adenopathy, rash, hepatitis, anemia, atypical lymphocytosis
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: CMV Mononucleosis in Immunocompetent Hosts
items.content: In immunocompetent hosts beyond the neonatal period, CMV mononucleosis is characterized by prolonged high fevers, profound fatigue, myalgias, headache, and splenomegaly. Unlike Epstein-Barr virus mononucleosis, exudative pharyngitis and cervical lymphadenopathy are rare. Laboratory findings include relative lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes, and serum aminotransferase and alkaline phosphatase levels may be elevated.
keyPoints: CMV mononucleosis most common in sexually active young adults
Incubation period: 20–60 days; duration: 2–6 weeks
Symptoms: prolonged high fevers, fatigue, myalgias, headache, splenomegaly
Exudative pharyngitis and cervical lymphadenopathy are rare
Laboratory findings: relative lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes
Serum aminotransferase and alkaline phosphatase levels often elevated
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: CMV Disease Post-Transplant: Risk Factors and Timing
items.content: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection is a significant risk in organ transplant recipients, leading to various clinical manifestations. The period of maximal risk for CMV disease occurs between 1 and 4 months post-transplantation. Factors increasing the likelihood of CMV disease include serologic mismatch (donor seropositive, recipient seronegative), high levels of immunosuppression, use of antilymphocyte antibodies, lack of anti-CMV prophylaxis, and co-infection with other pathogens.
keyPoints: CMV is a common complication in organ transplantation.
Maximal risk period for CMV disease: 1-4 months post-transplantation.
Risk factors include serologic mismatch, high immunosuppression, antilymphocyte antibodies, lack of prophylaxis, and co-infections.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: CMV in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients: Prophylaxis and Risk
items.content: In hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, CMV viremia occurs in about one-third of cases. The risk of severe disease is highest within the first 100 days post-transplantation and can be mitigated by prophylactic or preemptive antiviral therapy. Risk factors include certain immunosuppressive therapies, allogeneic grafts, acute graft-versus-host disease, older age, and recipient seropositivity.
keyPoints: CMV viremia occurs in ~1/3 of HSCT recipients.
Highest risk period: first 100 days post-transplantation.
Risk factors include specific immunosuppressive therapies, allogeneic grafts, acute GVHD, older age, and recipient seropositivity.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: CMV Retinitis in Immunocompromised Patients: Diagnosis and Risk
items.content: CMV retinitis is a significant cause of blindness, especially in patients with advanced AIDS. It presents as small, opaque, white areas of granular retinal necrosis that spread centrifugally, often accompanied by hemorrhages and vessel sheathing. Peripheral-blood CD4+ T cell counts below 50/μL in persons with HIV are predictive of severe disease and death due to CMV.
keyPoints: CMV retinitis is a major cause of blindness in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with advanced AIDS.
Early lesions: small, opaque, white areas of granular retinal necrosis spreading centrifugally.
CD4+ T cell counts < 50/μL predict severe CMV disease and death.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Preferred Methods for Diagnosing CMV Infection
items.content: CMV infection diagnosis relies on laboratory methods rather than clinical presentation alone. Quantitative nucleic acid testing (QNAT) by PCR is the most common method, using blood or other specimens. Detection of CMV DNA in cerebrospinal fluid aids in diagnosing encephalitis or polyradiculopathy.
keyPoints: Isolation of CMV or detection of its antigens/DNA is preferred for diagnosis.
Quantitative nucleic acid testing (QNAT) by PCR is the most common method.
CMV DNA in cerebrospinal fluid helps diagnose encephalitis/polyradiculopathy.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Serologic Assays and Their Limitations in CMV Diagnosis
items.content: Serologic assays detect antibodies to CMV but have limitations. IgG antibody levels may take up to 4 weeks post-primary infection to increase, making them less useful for acute diagnosis. IgM detection can indicate recent or active infection but is prone to false positives due to rheumatoid factors.
keyPoints: IgG antibodies may not be detectable for up to 4 weeks after primary CMV infection.
CMV-specific IgM can help diagnose recent/active infections but may yield false positives.
Serology is more useful for assessing risk in transplant recipients than diagnosing acute disease in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Universal Prophylaxis vs. Preemptive Therapy for CMV in Transplant Recipients
items.content: In organ transplant and HSCT recipients, prevention of CMV infection can be approached via universal prophylaxis or preemptive therapy. Universal prophylaxis involves administering antiviral drugs for a set period (often 3 to 6 months), while preemptive therapy entails monitoring patients weekly for CMV viremia and initiating treatment upon detection.
keyPoints: Universal prophylaxis with valganciclovir is more effective when given for 200 days compared to 100 days in CMV-seronegative kidney transplant recipients with seropositive donors.
Preemptive therapy is preferred in HSCT due to the bone marrow-suppressive effects of universal prophylaxis with ganciclovir/valganciclovir.
Letermovir provides excellent prophylaxis for higher-risk patients.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Prevention Strategies for CMV Transmission and End-Organ Disease
items.content: Several strategies are employed to prevent CMV transmission, particularly in high-risk groups. These include using CMV-seronegative or leukocyte-depleted blood products, exploring vaccines like the Triplex vaccine and mRNA-1647, and maintaining CD4+ T-cell counts above 100/μL with antiretroviral therapy for PWH.
keyPoints: CMV-seronegative or leukocyte-depleted blood decreases transfusion-associated transmission rates.
A CMV glycoprotein B vaccine reduced infection rates in a trial of 464 CMV-seronegative women, suggesting potential for reducing congenital infections.
For PWH, maintaining CD4+ T-cell counts above 100/μL is crucial to prevent CMV end-organ disease; primary prophylaxis with ganciclovir or valganciclovir is not recommended.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Ganciclovir and Valganciclovir for CMV Treatment
items.content: Ganciclovir, a guanosine derivative activated by viral phosphotransferase, selectively inhibits CMV DNA polymerase. It is effective in treating CMV retinitis or colitis with response rates of 70–90% among HIV patients. Valganciclovir, its oral prodrug, achieves similar blood levels to IV ganciclovir and offers the convenience of oral administration.
keyPoints: Ganciclovir has higher activity against CMV compared to acyclovir.
Response rates for HIV patients treated with ganciclovir are 70–90%.
Valganciclovir is orally bioavailable, with 60-70% absorption, and achieves similar blood levels as IV ganciclovir at a dose of 900 mg.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.title: Management of CMV Disease: Induction and Suppression
items.content: Treatment for CMV disease involves a 14- to 21-day induction course with ganciclovir or valganciclovir, followed by suppressive therapy if needed. Peripheral-blood neutropenia is common but manageable. Weekly monitoring of CMV NAT or antigenemia is recommended to assess virologic response.
keyPoints: Induction therapy: 5 mg/kg IV ganciclovir twice daily or 900 mg PO valganciclovir twice daily for 14-21 days.
Suppressive therapy may follow induction, using valganciclovir at 900 mg/d.
Peripheral-blood neutropenia occurs in about one-quarter of patients.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.title: Foscarnet Use and Considerations for CMV Treatment
items.content: Foscarnet, effective against ganciclovir-resistant CMV, inhibits DNA polymerase without requiring phosphorylation. It is used cautiously due to its toxicity profile, including renal dysfunction and electrolyte imbalances. Aggressive hydration and dose adjustments can mitigate some toxicities.
keyPoints: Foscarnet does not require phosphorylation for activity, making it effective against ganciclovir-resistant CMV.
Induction regimen: 60 mg/kg every 8 hours for 2 weeks; maintenance: 90–120 mg/kg once daily.
Toxicities include renal dysfunction and electrolyte imbalances; hydration and dose adjustments are necessary.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.title: Transmission and Integration of HHV-6
items.content: HHV-6 is transmitted primarily through saliva, with possible transmission via genital secretions. It can integrate into chromosomal telomeres in about 1% of people, leading to inherited chromosomally integrated HHV-6 (iciHHV-6), which affects offspring.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs mainly by saliva and possibly genital secretions.
HHV-6 DNA integrates into chromosomal telomeres in ~1% of individuals.
Offspring may inherit the viral genome, resulting in iciHHV-6.
Pregnant women with iciHHV-6 might have higher risks for pre-eclampsia or spontaneous abortion.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Risks of HHV-6 Infection
items.content: HHV-6 infection is common in infancy, with seropositivity rates approaching 80% by age 2. It can cause exanthem subitum (roseola infantum) and ~10–20% of febrile seizures without rash during infancy are due to HHV-6.
keyPoints: Infection typically occurs as maternal antibodies wane, peaking at 9–21 months.
Seropositivity rates approach 80% by age 24 months.
~10–20% of febrile seizures without rash in infancy are caused by HHV-6.
Exanthem subitum (roseola infantum) is characterized by fever followed by a rash.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.title: Complications of HHV-6 in Immunocompromised Hosts and Transplant Recipients
items.content: In immunocompromised individuals, HHV-6 can lead to severe conditions such as encephalitis and pneumonitis. In transplant recipients, it may cause graft dysfunction and is associated with increased mortality.
keyPoints: HHV-6 causes mononucleosis syndromes in older age groups.
In immunocompromised hosts, HHV-6 can lead to encephalitis, pneumonitis, and disseminated disease.
Acute limbic encephalitis in HSCT recipients is linked with high plasma loads of HHV-6 DNA.
HHV-6 infection in transplant recipients may result in graft dysfunction.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.title: Epidemiology of HHV-8 Infection and Associated Diseases
items.content: Human Herpesvirus Type 8 (HHV-8) is causally linked to Kaposi's sarcoma, primary effusion lymphomas, and multicentric Castleman disease. Its prevalence varies geographically, with higher rates in central and southern Africa compared to North America, Asia, and northern Europe. In high-prevalence areas, HHV-8 infection occurs in childhood, often through familial transmission via saliva. Conversely, in low-prevalence regions, it typically infects adults via sexual contact.
keyPoints: HHV-8 is associated with Kaposi's sarcoma (KS), primary effusion lymphomas, and multicentric Castleman disease.
Seropositivity rates are higher in central and southern Africa compared to North America, Asia, northern Europe.
In high-prevalence areas, HHV-8 infection occurs in childhood through familial transmission via saliva.
In low-prevalence regions, HHV-8 typically infects adults via sexual contact.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-8

items.title: Risk Factors and Transmission of HHV-8 Infection
items.content: HHV-8 infection is more common among men who have sex with men (MSM) with HIV, particularly in the southern United States. Risk factors include oral/anal or oral/penile sex and methamphetamine use. The virus may also be transmitted through organ transplantation, injection drug use, and blood transfusion, though such transmission is rare in the United States.
keyPoints: HHV-8 infection rates are higher among MSM with HIV in certain regions like the southern U.S.
Risk factors for HHV-8 include oral/anal or oral/penile sex and methamphetamine use.
Transmission routes include organ transplantation, injection drug use, and blood transfusion (rare in the U.S.).
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-8

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of CMV infection?
options: CMV causes only asymptomatic infections in adults.
CMV can cause both lytic and latent infections.
CMV replicates exclusively in fibroblasts in vivo.
items.explanation: CMV, like other herpesviruses, can cause either a lytic and productive or a latent infection.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the estimated overall prevalence of congenital CMV?
items.explanation: The overall prevalence of congenital CMV is estimated to be 0.67%.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: In which setting does CMV transmission commonly occur among young children?
items.explanation: CMV transmission can occur among young children in day-care centers.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is a common route of CMV transmission in late adolescence and young adulthood?
items.explanation: In late adolescence and young adulthood, CMV is often transmitted sexually.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which factor is associated with relatively severe congenital CMV disease?
options: Deficient capacity to produce precipitating antibodies and mount T cell responses
items.explanation: A deficient capacity to produce precipitating antibodies and to mount T cell responses is associated with relatively severe congenital CMV disease.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.question: What contributes to the development of rheumatoid factors during mononucleosis caused by CMV?
items.explanation: Polyclonal activation of B cells by CMV contributes to the development of rheumatoid factors during mononucleosis.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.question: What is a hallmark of primary infection with CMV in late childhood or adulthood?
options: Appearance of atypical lymphocytes in peripheral blood
items.explanation: The hallmark of primary CMV infection is the appearance of atypical lymphocytes, predominantly activated CD8+ T lymphocytes, in the peripheral blood.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.question: What is the most common presenting feature of congenital CMV infection?
items.explanation: Petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, and jaundice are the most common presenting features in congenital CMV infection.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a complication associated with perinatally acquired CMV infection?
items.explanation: Protracted interstitial pneumonitis is associated with perinatally acquired CMV infection, especially in premature infants.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of infants born to mothers with primary CMV infections during pregnancy develop clinical manifestations?
items.explanation: Of infants born to mothers with primary CMV infections, 5–20% develop clinical manifestations.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory abnormality is characteristic of CMV mononucleosis?
items.explanation: The characteristic laboratory abnormality of CMV mononucleosis is relative lymphocytosis in peripheral blood, with >10% atypical lymphocytes.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which period after transplantation poses the maximal risk for CMV disease without prophylaxis?
items.explanation: The period of maximal risk for CMV disease is between 1 and 4 months after transplantation without prophylaxis.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: In which transplant recipients is there a tendency for CMV hepatitis to occur?
items.explanation: CMV hepatitis tends to follow liver transplantation.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of gastrointestinal CMV involvement in organ transplant recipients?
items.explanation: Colitis is the most common clinical manifestation of gastrointestinal CMV involvement in organ transplant recipients.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which condition can acute flare-ups of CMV retinitis occur secondary to after starting highly active antiretroviral therapy?
items.explanation: Acute flare-ups of CMV retinitis may occur secondary to an immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) after starting highly active antiretroviral therapy.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which method is the preferred approach for diagnosing CMV infection?
options: Isolation of CMV or detection of its antigens or DNA in appropriate clinical specimens
Serologic assays detecting antibody to CMV
Detection of viremia by QNAT only
items.explanation: Isolation of CMV or detection of its antigens or DNA is the preferred diagnostic approach.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What does detection of viremia by QNAT or antigenemia testing predict better?
items.explanation: Detection of viremia by QNAT or antigenemia testing is a better predictor of acute infection.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Why might isolation of CMV from urine, stool, or saliva not indicate an acute infection?
options: CMV cannot be isolated from these specimens
Excretion from these sites may continue for months or years after illness
These methods are too slow to detect acute infections
Isolation from these sites always indicates chronic infection
items.explanation: Excretion of CMV from urine, stool, or saliva can persist long after the initial illness.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which method is more commonly employed for HSCT recipients to prevent CMV due to the bone marrow-suppressive effects of universal prophylaxis?
options: Primary prophylaxis with ganciclovir or valganciclovir
items.explanation: Preemptive therapy is more commonly employed in HSCT recipients due to the bone marrow-suppressive effects of universal prophylaxis with ganciclovir/valganciclovir.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: In CMV-seronegative kidney transplant recipients with seropositive donors, how long was valganciclovir prophylaxis found to be more effective?
items.explanation: Prophylaxis with valganciclovir was more effective when given for 200 days rather than 100 days.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended strategy to prevent CMV end-organ disease in people with HIV (PWH)?
options: Primary prophylaxis with ganciclovir or valganciclovir
Use of antiretroviral therapy sufficient to maintain CD4+ T-cell counts above 100/μL
items.explanation: For PWH, CMV end-organ disease is best prevented by using antiretroviral therapy sufficient to maintain CD4+ T-cell counts above 100/μL.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with resistance to ganciclovir in CMV infections?
items.explanation: Resistance to ganciclovir is usually related to mutations in the CMV UL97 gene.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.question: What is a common adverse effect of foscarnet therapy for CMV infections?
items.explanation: Foscarnet causes considerable toxicity, including renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.question: Which drug is an orally bioavailable prodrug that metabolizes to ganciclovir?
items.explanation: Valganciclovir is an orally bioavailable prodrug that is rapidly metabolized to ganciclovir.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.question: For which CMV infection scenario is prolonged prevention regimen recommended?
options: CMV pneumonia in HSCT recipients with no prior antiviral exposure
PWH with persistently low CD4+ T cell counts
Infants with congenital CMV infection receiving short treatment courses
items.explanation: In many PWH, those with persistently low CD4+ T cell counts, and those with CMV disease, prolonged prevention regimens may be recommended.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.question: Which two genetically distinct variants of HHV-6 are recognized?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that there are two genetically distinct variants of HHV-6, namely HHV-6A and HHV-6B.

items.explanation: According to the passage, about ~1% of newborns are infected with HHV-6 congenitally.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.question: Which clinical condition is associated with high plasma loads of HHV-6 DNA in HSCT recipients?

options: B lymphocytes, macrophages, endothelial and epithelial cells

items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: CMV is readily spread by casual contact.
items.explanation: CMV is not readily spread by casual contact but requires repeated or prolonged intimate exposure.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: CMV can reactivate and cause disease when the host's T cell responses are compromised.
items.explanation: Latent CMV can reactivate to cause various syndromes if the host's T cell responses become compromised.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Cytomegalic cells are smaller than surrounding cells and lack intranuclear inclusions.
items.explanation: Cytomegalic cells are larger than surrounding cells and often contain an 8- to 10-μm intranuclear inclusion with a clear halo.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ■ PATHOGENESIS | PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Most congenital CMV infections are clinically apparent at birth.
items.explanation: Most congenital CMV infections are clinically inapparent at birth.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: CMV mononucleosis is characterized by exudative pharyngitis and cervical lymphadenopathy.
items.explanation: In CMV mononucleosis, exudative pharyngitis and cervical lymphadenopathy are rare.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: CMV infection is more likely to be clinically significant in cases of reactivation than primary infection.
items.explanation: Reactivation infection, although common, is less likely than primary infection to be clinically significant.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: CMV retinitis can lead to blindness in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: CMV retinitis is an important cause of blindness in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with advanced AIDS.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Serologic assays are recommended to diagnose acute disease in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Serology is not recommended for diagnosing acute disease in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Detection of CMV DNA in cerebrospinal fluid can be useful in diagnosing CMV encephalitis.
items.explanation: CMV DNA in cerebrospinal fluid is indeed helpful for diagnosing CMV encephalitis.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: A conditionally replication-defective CMV vaccine (V160) reduced the incidence of primary CMV infection in a phase 2 clinical trial.
items.explanation: Three doses of V160 did not reduce the incidence of primary CMV infection compared with placebo.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: CMV immune globulin is commonly used in the era of multiple effective antiviral agents.
items.explanation: CMV immune globulin is used much less often due to the availability of multiple effective antiviral agents.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Foscarnet requires phosphorylation to be active.
items.explanation: Foscarnet does not require phosphorylation to be active.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.statement: Valganciclovir is less effective than IV ganciclovir for CMV induction and prevention.
items.explanation: Valganciclovir appears to be as effective as IV ganciclovir for both CMV induction and prevention regimens.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.statement: HHV-7 is primarily transmitted through breast milk.
items.explanation: The passage states that saliva is presumed to be the principal source of infection for HHV-7, with breast milk and cervical secretions also potentially carrying the virus.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.statement: HHV-6 DNA can integrate into chromosomal telomeres.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that HHV-6 DNA integrates into chromosomal telomeres in several cell types.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.statement: HHV-8 infection rates are uniformly distributed worldwide.
items.explanation: HHV-8 seropositivity varies geographically, with higher rates in certain areas like central and southern Africa.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-8

items.statement: Transmission of HHV-8 can occur via organ transplantation.
items.explanation: Transmission of HHV-8 may be associated with organ transplantation, although it is rare in the United States.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-8

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: CMV reactivation is more frequent in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reason: T lymphocyte-mediated immunity is compromised in these patients.
items.explanation: Reactivation syndromes develop more frequently when T lymphocyte-mediated immunity is compromised.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.reason: CMV is a $ eta$-herpesvirus with double-stranded DNA.
items.explanation: While CMV can cause congenital defects, the reason provided does not explain this capability.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6, 7, and 8 | DEFINITION | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: CMV infection in immunocompromised hosts can be fatal.
items.reason: Survivors of CMV infection may have recurrent episodes of fever and malaise, sometimes associated with autonomic nervous system dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason is not a direct explanation for the fatality in immunocompromised hosts.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Infants infected at or after delivery with CMV often develop severe symptoms.
items.reason: The great majority of infants infected perinatally remain asymptomatic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because most perinatal infections are asymptomatic, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: In hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients, the risk of severe CMV disease is greatest in the first 100 days after transplantation.
items.reason: Risk factors include certain types of immunosuppressive therapy and acute graft-versus-host disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why the risk is greatest in the first 100 days.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: CMV infection rarely causes meningoencephalitis in otherwise healthy individuals.
items.reason: Two forms of CMV encephalitis are seen in persons with HIV, resembling HIV encephalitis or presenting as ventriculoencephalitis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why CMV rarely causes meningoencephalitis in healthy individuals.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Maribavir is used for resistant/refractory CMV infections.
items.reason: It was shown in a phase 3 trial to be more effective and have less toxicity compared with alternative therapies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why maribavir is used for resistant/refractory CMV infections.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.assertion: Letermovir is recommended for treatment during active CMV infection.
items.reason: It induces rapid development of resistance when used during active infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because letermovir is not recommended for treatment during active infection due to the reason provided.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · Cytomegalovirus Infection

items.assertion: Inherited chromosomally integrated HHV-6 (iciHHV-6) can lead to offspring carrying the viral genome in every nucleated cell.
items.reason: HHV-6 DNA integrates into chromosomal telomeres in several cell types, including germ cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the integration of HHV-6 DNA into chromosomal telomeres explains why offspring may carry the viral genome.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.assertion: Limbic encephalitis in HSCT recipients is associated with high plasma loads of HHV-6 DNA.
items.reason: High plasma loads of HHV-6 DNA are linked to increased all-cause mortality rates and graft dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why limbic encephalitis is associated with high plasma loads of HHV-6 DNA.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-6 AND HHV-7

items.assertion: The frequent association of AIDS and Kaposi's sarcoma in certain populations was influenced by concurrent epidemics.
items.reason: Concurrent epidemics of HIV-1 and HHV-8 infections among MSM in the late 1970s and early 1980s led to this association.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cytomegalovirus and Human Herpesvirus Types 6 7 and 8 · HHV-8
